draft.introduction: Juvenal’s Satire 6 is the longest and most controversial of his satires, a sweeping attack on the vices of Roman women and the institution of marriage. Its rhetorical excess has made it both foundational to and problematic within the satiric tradition.

translation.text: I believe that in the days of Saturn, Chastity still lingered on the earth and was sometimes seen. The cold cave provided a common home, enclosing fire and household gods and herds and their owners together. A mountain wife would spread her bed of leaves and straw and the skins of neighboring beasts. She was not like you, Cynthia, nor you whose bright eyes were clouded by the death of a sparrow.
translation.original_text: Credo Pudicitiam Saturno rege moratam in terris visamque diu

translator_notes.note: The satire’s opening invocation of a lost Golden Age of sexual purity follows Hesiod’s degenerative scheme. Modern scholars debate whether Juvenal attacks women specifically or uses misogynistic convention to satirize Roman decadence generally. The poem’s influence on medieval and Renaissance attitudes toward women was immense.
